sourceMovie.title: The Lego Movie
sourceMovie.whyPeopleLoveIt: The Lego Movie (2014) stuck because it commits to its animation premise without apologizing—viewers remember set pieces and momentum as much as plot. Phil Lord's control of tone keeps the ride coherent. The animation and family blend is the engine: it lets the film shift registers without feeling like two different movies stitched together.

recommendations.title: Onward
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: If The Lego Movie clicked for you, Onward (2020) is a strong next stop: both trade in animation and family, with comic timing and sharper banter. Expect a different story, but a similar willingness to go big when the moment demands it.

recommendations.title: Toy Story
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: If The Lego Movie clicked for you, Toy Story (1995) is a strong next stop: both trade in animation and family, with comic timing and sharper banter. Expect a different story, but a similar willingness to go big when the moment demands it.

faq.question: What should I watch after The Lego Movie?
faq.answer: Try Onward, Toy Story, Big Hero 6—each shares DNA with The Lego Movie while changing the setting enough to feel fresh.

faq.question: What genre is The Lego Movie?
faq.answer: The Lego Movie (2014) is best described as Animation, Family, Adventure, Comedy, Fantasy. Stores and algorithms use different labels, but that mix is what fans actually recognize on a rewatch.

faq.question: Is The Lego Movie part of a franchise?
faq.answer: Yes—it's part of the "The Lego Movie Collection" storyline. You can enjoy it standalone, but sequels and tie-ins build on characters and continuity established here.
